1	// SPDX-License-Identifier: BSD-3-Clause
2	// Copyright (c) 2025-2026 Kevin Barnard and minix.rs Contributors
3	//! Kernel-call numbers â€” the `m_type` values for `SENDREC`s addressed to the
4	//! `SYSTEM` task.
5	//!
6	//! Numbering convention follows MINIX 3 `include/minix/com.h` (`KERNEL_CALL`
7	//! base, contiguous offsets). Only the 14 calls needed by Phase 2 are
8	//! defined; more are added as later phases come online.
9	
10	/// Base for kernel-call numbers. Matches MINIX 3 `KERNEL_CALL`.
11	pub const KERNEL_CALL: i32 = 0x600;
12	
13	#[allow(clippy::identity_op)] // explicit `+ 0` keeps the table aligned visually
14	pub const SYS_GETINFO: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 0;
15	pub const SYS_PRIVCTL: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 1;
16	pub const SYS_FORK: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 2;
17	pub const SYS_EXEC: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 3;
18	pub const SYS_EXIT: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 4;
19	pub const SYS_COPY: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 5;
20	pub const SYS_SAFECOPY: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 6;
21	pub const SYS_IRQCTL: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 7;
22	pub const SYS_VMCTL: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 8;
23	pub const SYS_SCHEDULE: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 9;
24	pub const SYS_SETALARM: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 10;
25	pub const SYS_TIMES: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 11;
26	pub const SYS_DIAGCTL: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 12;
27	pub const SYS_SETGRANT: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 13;
28	/// Scheduler claim/release. A user-space scheduler (SCHED) calls this to take a
29	/// target proc under its management (`target.scheduler = caller`) or hand it
30	/// back to the kernel scheduler (`SCHEDCTL_FLAG_KERNEL`). Made real in slice 4.3
31	/// alongside `SYS_SCHEDULE`; payload layout mirrors `SYS_VMCTL` (flags in
32	/// `0..4`, target endpoint in `4..8`).
33	pub const SYS_SCHEDCTL: i32 = KERNEL_CALL + 14;
34	
35	/// `SYS_SCHEDCTL` flag: revert the target to kernel scheduling
36	/// (`target.scheduler = NONE`). Absent â†’ the caller claims the target as its
37	/// own scheduler. Matches MINIX 3 `SCHEDCTL_FLAG_KERNEL` (`include/minix/com.h`).
38	pub const SCHEDCTL_FLAG_KERNEL: i32 = 1 << 0;
39	
40	/// Number of kernel calls defined through Phase 4. Slice 4.3 makes
41	/// `SYS_SCHEDULE` real and adds `SYS_SCHEDCTL`, bringing the count to 15.
42	pub const NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE4: usize = 15;
43	
44	/// One-slice alias for the previous phase's count, kept so slice 4.3's existing
45	/// range guards (the VM/DS/SEF "above the last kernel call" asserts) keep
46	/// compiling unchanged. Dropped in slice 4.4, exactly as `_PHASE2` was dropped
47	/// one slice after `_PHASE3` landed.
48	pub const NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3: usize = NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE4;
49	
50	/// Size of the privilege-table kernel-call mask, in bits. Sized as a single
51	/// `u32` chunk (32 slots) to leave headroom past Phase 4's 15 calls while
52	/// keeping the bitmap a single word per privilege slot.
53	pub const NR_SYS_CALLS: usize = 32;
54	
55	const _: () = assert!(NR_SYS_CALLS >= NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE4);
56	const _: () = assert!(NR_SYS_CALLS.is_multiple_of(32));
57	
58	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
59	// `SYS_GETINFO` request sub-types.
60	//
61	// `SYS_GETINFO` is a multi-purpose introspection call: the request sub-type
62	// in the first 4 bytes of the message payload selects what the kernel reports
63	// back. Numbering matches MINIX 3 `include/minix/sysinfo.h` so the same wire
64	// values can be reused once musl + servers land.
65	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
66	
67	/// `SYS_GETINFO` request: return the caller's endpoint, priv flags, init
68	/// flags, and process name. The kernel writes the reply into the payload of
69	/// the request message in-place; on return `m_type == OK`.
70	pub const GET_WHOAMI: i32 = 12;
71	
72	/// Length of the `name` field in the `GET_WHOAMI` reply payload. minix.rs uses
73	/// the kernel's own `PROC_NAME_LEN` here rather than MINIX 3's 44-byte field â€”
74	/// the name is only used for debug/log output and the kernel never stores more
75	/// than 16 bytes per slot.
76	pub const SYS_GETINFO_NAME_LEN: usize = 16;
77	
78	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
79	// `SYS_VMCTL` subcalls.
80	//
81	// `SYS_VMCTL` mediates all user-space page-table changes: the kernel owns the
82	// physical frame allocator and every unsafe PTE write, and VM (slice 3.4)
83	// drives policy by issuing these subcalls. The subcall selector lives in the
84	// first 4 bytes of the message payload (same convention as `GET_WHOAMI`); the
85	// target process is named by an endpoint in the next 4 bytes (`SELF` allowed).
86	// Numbers start at 1 so a zeroed payload (subcall 0) is an obvious "invalid".
87	// These are minix.rs-specific â€” MINIX 3's VMCTL subcall set differs because its
88	// frame allocator lives in VM, not the kernel.
89	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
90	
91	/// Allocate a fresh zeroed frame and map it at `vaddr` in the target's
92	/// address space with the requested protection. The allocated PA is returned
93	/// in the reply payload. (The kernel allocates because the frame allocator is
94	/// kernel-side; VM supplies only `vaddr` + protection.)
95	pub const VMCTL_PT_MAP: i32 = 1;
96	/// Unmap `vaddr` in the target's address space and free the backing frame.
97	pub const VMCTL_PT_UNMAP: i32 = 2;
98	/// Clear the target's pending page fault and make it runnable again.
99	pub const VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT: i32 = 3;
100	/// Read the target's recorded page-fault state (addr/flags/ip) into the reply.
101	/// Valid only while the target is blocked on a page fault.
102	pub const VMCTL_GET_PAGEFAULT: i32 = 4;
103	/// Inhibit scheduling of the target while VM mutates its address space.
104	pub const VMCTL_VMINHIBIT_SET: i32 = 5;
105	/// Release a prior `VMCTL_VMINHIBIT_SET`.
106	pub const VMCTL_VMINHIBIT_CLEAR: i32 = 6;
107	
108	/// Number of `SYS_VMCTL` subcalls. Locks the dispatch-match coverage in
109	/// `system::do_vmctl` via a const-assert.
110	pub const NR_VMCTL_SUBCALLS: usize = 6;
111	
112	// `VMCTL_PT_MAP` protection bits (message payload, `vaddr`-adjacent word).
113	/// EL0 may write the mapped page.
114	pub const VMCTL_PROT_WRITE: i32 = 1 << 0;
115	/// EL0 may execute from the mapped page.
116	pub const VMCTL_PROT_EXEC: i32 = 1 << 1;
117	
118	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
119	// VM server request numbers â€” `m_type` values for messages addressed to VM.
120	//
121	// These are *server IPC requests*, not kernel calls, so they live in their own
122	// range distinct from `KERNEL_CALL` (`0x600`). The kernel originates
123	// `VM_PAGEFAULT` on a faulting process's behalf (slice 3.4); later slices add
124	// `VM_BRK` / `VM_MMAP` / `VM_MUNMAP`. Numbering is minix.rs-specific (MINIX 3's VM request set
125	// differs because its frame allocator lives in VM, not the kernel).
126	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
127	
128	/// Base for VM server request `m_type` values.
129	pub const VM_RQ_BASE: i32 = 0xC00;
130	
131	/// Kernel â†’ VM: a process page-faulted. `m_source` identifies the faulting
132	/// process; the payload carries the fault address (`0..8`, u64) and fault
133	/// flags (`8..12`, u32). VM resolves it via `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_MAP)` +
134	/// `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT)`.
135	pub const VM_PAGEFAULT: i32 = VM_RQ_BASE;
136	
137	/// EL0 â†’ VM: set the caller's program break to `new_break` (payload `0..8`,
138	/// u64). VM grows or creates the caller's heap region to `[HEAP_BASE,
139	/// new_break)`; pages fault in lazily on first touch (no eager mapping). The
140	/// reply carries `m_type = OK` and the resulting break in payload `0..8`, or a
141	/// negative error in `m_type`. (slice 3.5)
142	pub const VM_BRK: i32 = VM_RQ_BASE + 1;
143	
144	/// EL0 â†’ VM: anonymous mmap. The caller requests `len` bytes (payload `0..8`,
145	/// u64); VM page-aligns the length, picks a free VA from the caller's mmap bump
146	/// arena, records an `Mmap` region, and replies with the chosen base address in
147	/// payload `0..8` and `m_type = OK`. Like `mmap(NULL, len, â€¦)`: VM chooses the
148	/// address. Pages fault in lazily on first touch (no eager mapping). On failure
149	/// the negative error is in `m_type` (`EINVAL` for a zero or overflowing
150	/// length, `ENOMEM` when no region slot is free). (slice 3.6)
151	pub const VM_MMAP: i32 = VM_RQ_BASE + 2;
152	
153	/// EL0 â†’ VM: unmap a prior mmap. The caller passes the base address (payload
154	/// `0..8`, u64) and length (payload `8..16`, u64). VM page-aligns the range,
155	/// drops the matching `Mmap` region, and unmaps each backing page via
156	/// `SYS_VMCTL(VMCTL_PT_UNMAP)` (a never-faulted page returns a harmless
157	/// `EINVAL` from the kernel, which VM ignores). The reply carries
158	/// `m_type = OK`, or `EINVAL` in `m_type` if no `Mmap` region matches the base
159	/// address. (slice 3.6)
160	pub const VM_MUNMAP: i32 = VM_RQ_BASE + 3;
161	
162	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
163	// DS (Data Store) server request numbers â€” `m_type` values for messages
164	// addressed to the DS server.
165	//
166	// DS is a nameâ†’endpoint registry: every server publishes its own endpoint at
167	// init (slice 4.2) so others can look each other up without hard-coding boot
168	// proc numbers. These are *server IPC requests* like the VM range, so they live
169	// in their own range, distinct from `KERNEL_CALL` (`0x600`), the VM request
170	// range (`VM_RQ_BASE = 0xC00`), and the SEF control range (`SEF_RQ_BASE =
171	// 0xD00`), and stay below the IPC `NOTIFY_MESSAGE` marker (`0x1000`) so neither
172	// a server's `m_type` dispatcher nor the SEF classifier can ever misroute.
173	//
174	// The key (a NUL-padded server name) travels inline in the request payload
175	// (`0..SYS_GETINFO_NAME_LEN`); no grants / cross-AS copy are needed because the
176	// kernel copies the whole 96-byte payload on delivery. An endpoint value rides
177	// in payload `16..20` (i32, native-endian) only on `DS_RETRIEVE` replies;
178	// `DS_PUBLISH` registers the caller's kernel-stamped `m_source` (a process can
179	// only publish itself), so no endpoint is sent in a publish request. Numbering
180	// is minix.rs-specific.
181	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
182	
183	/// Base for DS server request `m_type` values.
184	pub const DS_RQ_BASE: i32 = 0xE00;
185	
186	/// Server â†’ DS: publish the *caller's own* endpoint under the key in payload
187	/// `0..SYS_GETINFO_NAME_LEN`. DS records the caller's kernel-stamped `m_source`,
188	/// not a value from the payload, so a process can only publish itself and can
189	/// never spoof another server's endpoint. Re-publishing the same key updates the
190	/// stored endpoint. Reply `m_type = OK`, or `EINVAL` (empty key) / `ENOMEM`
191	/// (registry full).
192	pub const DS_PUBLISH: i32 = DS_RQ_BASE;
193	
194	/// Client â†’ DS: look up the endpoint for the key in payload `0..NAME_LEN`.
195	/// Reply `m_type = OK` with the endpoint in payload `16..20` (i32), or
196	/// `ESRCH` if the key is not registered.
197	pub const DS_RETRIEVE: i32 = DS_RQ_BASE + 1;
198	
199	/// Client â†’ DS: test whether the key in payload `0..NAME_LEN` is registered.
200	/// Reply `m_type = OK` with a status in payload `16..20` (i32: 1 = present,
201	/// 0 = absent) â€” absence is a status, not an error, so a `CHECK` never aborts
202	/// the caller's SENDREC.
203	pub const DS_CHECK: i32 = DS_RQ_BASE + 2;
204	
205	/// Number of DS server requests defined so far. Locks the dispatch-match
206	/// coverage in the DS server the way `NR_VMCTL_SUBCALLS` locks `do_vmctl`.
207	pub const NR_DS_REQUESTS: usize = 3;
208	
209	// The DS range sits strictly above the SEF range (0xD00..0xD01) so a server's
210	// `m_type` dispatcher and the SEF classifier can never collide, and stays
211	// below the NOTIFY marker.
212	const _: () = assert!(DS_RQ_BASE > SEF_RQ_BASE + (NR_SEF_MSGS as i32 - 1));
213	const _: () = assert!(DS_RQ_BASE + (NR_DS_REQUESTS as i32 - 1) < crate::ipc_const::NOTIFY_MESSAGE);
214	
215	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
216	// SEF (System Event Framework) control message numbers â€” `m_type` values the
217	// server runtime (`server-rt`) intercepts before handing traffic to a server.
218	//
219	// These live in their own range distinct from `KERNEL_CALL` (`0x600`) and the
220	// VM request range (`VM_RQ_BASE = 0xC00`), and stay below the IPC
221	// `NOTIFY_MESSAGE` marker (`0x1000`), so neither a server's `m_type`
222	// dispatcher nor the SEF classifier can ever misroute. Numbering is
223	// minix.rs-specific (MINIX 3 carries these inside `lib/libsys/sef.c` request
224	// types rather than `com.h`).
225	//
226	// The RS heartbeat ("ping") deliberately gets NO number here: it is delivered
227	// as a NOTIFY, so it arrives with `m_type == NOTIFY_MESSAGE` and is keyed on
228	// `m_source == RS` instead (see `server-rt`'s `classify`). Do not add a
229	// `SEF_PING` â€” there is no payload room in a NOTIFY to carry one anyway.
230	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
231	
232	/// Base for SEF control message `m_type` values.
233	pub const SEF_RQ_BASE: i32 = 0xD00;
234	
235	/// RS â†’ server: run the registered fresh-init callback. (Re-init / live-update
236	/// variants are deferred past Phase 4.)
237	pub const SEF_INIT: i32 = SEF_RQ_BASE;
238	
239	/// PM/RS â†’ server: deliver a signal. The signal number is in payload `0..4`
240	/// (i32, native-endian); `server-rt` dispatches it to the registered signal
241	/// handler.
242	pub const SEF_SIGNAL: i32 = SEF_RQ_BASE + 1;
243	
244	/// Number of SEF control messages defined so far. Locks the classifier's
245	/// coverage in `server-rt` the way `NR_VMCTL_SUBCALLS` locks `do_vmctl`.
246	pub const NR_SEF_MSGS: usize = 2;
247	
248	// The SEF range sits strictly above the VM request range (0xC00..0xC03) so a
249	// server's `m_type` dispatcher and the SEF classifier can never collide.
250	const _: () = assert!(SEF_RQ_BASE > VM_RQ_BASE + 3);
251	const _: () = assert!(SEF_RQ_BASE < crate::ipc_const::NOTIFY_MESSAGE);
252	
253	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
254	// SCHED (scheduler) server request numbers â€” `m_type` values for messages
255	// addressed to the user-space SCHED server (slice 4.3).
256	//
257	// `SCHEDULING_NO_QUANTUM` is kernel-originated: when a SCHED-scheduled proc
258	// exhausts its quantum, the kernel sends it (with `m_source` = the preempted
259	// proc, so SCHED knows which proc to reschedule), exactly as it originates
260	// `VM_PAGEFAULT` for a faulter. The other three are PM/RS â†’ SCHED requests
261	// (claim/release/renice a managed proc). Like the VM/DS ranges these are
262	// *server IPC requests*, not kernel calls, so they live in their own range
263	// distinct from `KERNEL_CALL` (`0x600`), VM (`0xC00`), SEF (`0xD00`), and DS
264	// (`0xE00`), and stay below the IPC `NOTIFY_MESSAGE` marker (`0x1000`) so the
265	// SEF classifier (which returns `Application` for them) can never misroute.
266	// Numbering is minix.rs-specific (MINIX 3 carries `SCHEDULING_*` in `com.h`).
267	// ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
268	
269	/// Base for SCHED server request `m_type` values.
270	pub const SCHED_RQ_BASE: i32 = 0xF00;
271	
272	/// Kernel â†’ SCHED: the proc identified by `m_source` used up its full quantum.
273	/// SCHED applies its policy and re-admits the proc via `SYS_SCHEDULE`. Carries
274	/// no payload â€” `m_source` is the whole request.
275	pub const SCHEDULING_NO_QUANTUM: i32 = SCHED_RQ_BASE;
276	
277	/// PM/RS â†’ SCHED: start scheduling a proc. The target endpoint is in payload
278	/// `0..4` (i32), the initial priority in `4..8` (i32), and the quantum (ms) in
279	/// `8..12` (i32). SCHED claims the target via `SYS_SCHEDCTL` and assigns the
280	/// initial priority/quantum via `SYS_SCHEDULE`. (Driven by PM/RS from slice 4.5+.)
281	pub const SCHEDULING_START: i32 = SCHED_RQ_BASE + 1;
282	
283	/// PM/RS â†’ SCHED: stop scheduling the target (payload `0..4`, i32). SCHED hands
284	/// it back to the kernel scheduler via `SYS_SCHEDCTL(SCHEDCTL_FLAG_KERNEL)`.
285	pub const SCHEDULING_STOP: i32 = SCHED_RQ_BASE + 2;
286	
287	/// PM/RS â†’ SCHED: change the target's nice value. Target endpoint in payload
288	/// `0..4` (i32), new priority in `4..8` (i32). SCHED records it and applies it
289	/// via `SYS_SCHEDULE`.
290	pub const SCHEDULING_SET_NICE: i32 = SCHED_RQ_BASE + 3;
291	
292	/// Number of SCHED server requests defined so far. Locks the dispatch-match
293	/// coverage in the SCHED server the way `NR_DS_REQUESTS` locks the DS server.
294	pub const NR_SCHED_MSGS: usize = 4;
295	
296	// The SCHED range sits strictly above the DS range (0xE00..0xE02) and below the
297	// NOTIFY marker, so neither a server's `m_type` dispatcher nor the SEF
298	// classifier can ever collide with it.
299	const _: () = assert!(SCHED_RQ_BASE > DS_RQ_BASE + (NR_DS_REQUESTS as i32 - 1));
300	const _: () =
301	    assert!(SCHED_RQ_BASE + (NR_SCHED_MSGS as i32 - 1) < crate::ipc_const::NOTIFY_MESSAGE);
302	
303	#[cfg(test)]
304	mod tests {
305	    use super::*;
306	
307	    #[test]
308	    fn calls_are_contiguous_from_base() {
309	        let calls = [
310	            SYS_GETINFO,
311	            SYS_PRIVCTL,
312	            SYS_FORK,
313	            SYS_EXEC,
314	            SYS_EXIT,
315	            SYS_COPY,
316	            SYS_SAFECOPY,
317	            SYS_IRQCTL,
318	            SYS_VMCTL,
319	            SYS_SCHEDULE,
320	            SYS_SETALARM,
321	            SYS_TIMES,
322	            SYS_DIAGCTL,
323	            SYS_SETGRANT,
324	            SYS_SCHEDCTL,
325	        ];
326	        for (i, call) in calls.iter().enumerate() {
327	            assert_eq!(*call, KERNEL_CALL + i as i32);
328	        }
329	        assert_eq!(calls.len(), NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE4);
330	    }
331	
332	    #[test]
333	    fn kernel_call_base_matches_minix3() {
334	        assert_eq!(KERNEL_CALL, 0x600);
335	    }
336	
337	    #[test]
338	    fn get_whoami_matches_minix3() {
339	        // Pinned by MINIX 3 include/minix/sysinfo.h; servers / musl wrappers
340	        // built later in the project depend on this value.
341	        assert_eq!(GET_WHOAMI, 12);
342	    }
343	
344	    #[test]
345	    fn vmctl_subcalls_are_contiguous_from_one() {
346	        // Subcall 0 is reserved as "invalid" (a zeroed payload). The six
347	        // real subcalls are 1..=6 and distinct; `NR_VMCTL_SUBCALLS` locks
348	        // the dispatch coverage in `system::do_vmctl`.
349	        let subcalls = [
350	            VMCTL_PT_MAP,
351	            VMCTL_PT_UNMAP,
352	            VMCTL_CLEAR_PAGEFAULT,
353	            VMCTL_GET_PAGEFAULT,
354	            VMCTL_VMINHIBIT_SET,
355	            VMCTL_VMINHIBIT_CLEAR,
356	        ];
357	        for (i, sc) in subcalls.iter().enumerate() {
358	            assert_eq!(*sc, 1 + i as i32);
359	        }
360	        assert_eq!(subcalls.len(), NR_VMCTL_SUBCALLS);
361	    }
362	
363	    #[test]
364	    fn vm_pagefault_distinct_from_kernel_calls_and_notify() {
365	        // VM requests must not collide with the KERNEL_CALL range, the IPC
366	        // NOTIFY_MESSAGE marker, or any SYS_* number â€” a server dispatcher
367	        // keys on m_type and a collision would misroute.
368	        assert_eq!(VM_PAGEFAULT, VM_RQ_BASE);
369	        assert!(VM_PAGEFAULT > KERNEL_CALL + NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3 as i32);
370	        assert_ne!(VM_PAGEFAULT, crate::ipc_const::NOTIFY_MESSAGE);
371	    }
372	
373	    #[test]
374	    fn vm_brk_follows_pagefault_in_request_range() {
375	        // VM_BRK is the second VM server request, contiguous after VM_PAGEFAULT.
376	        // It must stay distinct from the page-fault request, the KERNEL_CALL
377	        // range, and the NOTIFY marker so VM's m_type dispatcher can't misroute.
378	        assert_eq!(VM_BRK, VM_RQ_BASE + 1);
379	        assert_ne!(VM_BRK, VM_PAGEFAULT);
380	        assert!(VM_BRK > KERNEL_CALL + NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3 as i32);
381	        assert_ne!(VM_BRK, crate::ipc_const::NOTIFY_MESSAGE);
382	    }
383	
384	    #[test]
385	    fn vm_mmap_follows_brk_in_request_range() {
386	        // VM_MMAP is the third VM server request, contiguous after VM_BRK.
387	        assert_eq!(VM_MMAP, VM_RQ_BASE + 2);
388	        assert_ne!(VM_MMAP, VM_PAGEFAULT);
389	        assert_ne!(VM_MMAP, VM_BRK);
390	        assert!(VM_MMAP > KERNEL_CALL + NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3 as i32);
391	        assert_ne!(VM_MMAP, crate::ipc_const::NOTIFY_MESSAGE);
392	    }
393	
394	    #[test]
395	    fn vm_munmap_follows_mmap_in_request_range() {
396	        // VM_MUNMAP is the fourth VM server request, contiguous after VM_MMAP.
397	        // Each VM request must stay distinct from the others, the KERNEL_CALL
398	        // range, and the NOTIFY marker so VM's m_type dispatcher can't misroute.
399	        assert_eq!(VM_MUNMAP, VM_RQ_BASE + 3);
400	        assert_ne!(VM_MUNMAP, VM_MMAP);
401	        assert_ne!(VM_MUNMAP, VM_BRK);
402	        assert_ne!(VM_MUNMAP, VM_PAGEFAULT);

+/// `0..4` (i32), signal number in `4..8` (i32, `1..NSIG`). The kernel records
+/// the signal in the target's pending bitmap (`cause_sig`) and notifies PM,
+/// which drains via `SYS_GETKSIG` / `SYS_ENDKSIG`. This is the MINIX 3

lines: +// `SYS_PRIVCTL` (real as of slice 4.5) sets up a target proc's privilege
+// slot. The target endpoint lives in payload `0..4` and the subcode in `4..8`
+// (both i32, the same target-first convention as `SYS_SCHEDULE`). Numbers
+// start at 1 so a zeroed payload is an obvious "invalid" (the `VMCTL_*`

+/// an empty kernel-call mask â€” ordinary user processes make no kernel calls.

SYS_KILL/SYS_GETKSIG/SYS_ENDKSIG call numbers (NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE4
15 -> 18, overdue NR_KERN_CALLS_PHASE3 alias dropped), PRIVCTL_SET_USER
subcode, PM_RQ_BASE 0x700 request range (PM_GETPID), signal.rs with the
POSIX numbers PM raises, and shared STUB_A..E proc numbers so PM's mproc
